title: UCB — Epilepsy — knowledge doc dravet

frontmatter.focus: Dravet Syndrome / Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome / CDKL5 Deficiency Disorder

html:
Market Context: Fintepla (Fenfluramine) in Rare Developmental & Epileptic Encephalopathies

Product: FINTEPLA (fenfluramine) oral solution Manufacturer: UCB (acquired via Zogenix for $1.9B, 2022) Indications: Dravet syndrome (DS), Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (LGS) — seizures in patients >=2 years of age Pipeline indication: CDKL5 deficiency disorder (Phase 3 GEMZ positive, filing planned) 2025 global revenue: EUR427 million (~$501M USD), +30% YoY Last updated: April 2026

1. Dravet Syndrome — Disease Deep Dive

1.1 Epidemiology and Disease Burden

Dravet syndrome (DS) is one of the most severe forms of childhood-onset epilepsy:

  • Incidence: ~1 in 15,700 live births; estimated 3,000-8,000 patients in the US
  • Genetic basis: ~80% of cases caused by de novo mutations in the SCN1A gene (sodium channel Nav1.1)
  • Age of onset: Typically within the first year of life (5-8 months), beginning with prolonged febrile seizures
  • Seizure types: Convulsive (generalized tonic-clonic, hemiclonic), myoclonic, absence, focal seizures — often triggered by fever, illness, photosensitivity, overexertion
  • Status epilepticus: 40-70% of DS patients experience status epilepticus during their lifetime — a medical emergency
  • SUDEP risk: Patients with DS have significantly higher risk of sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP) compared to the general epilepsy population
  • Comorbidities: Intellectual disability (>80%), behavioral challenges, gait abnormalities, growth/nutrition issues, sleep disturbance, autonomic dysfunction
  • Mortality: ~15-20% mortality by age 20 in historical cohorts; modern treatments have improved survival

1.2 Treatment Landscape for Dravet Syndrome

Dravet syndrome is uniquely challenging because many standard ASMs can WORSEN seizures:

Drugs that WORSEN DS seizures (avoid):

  • Sodium channel blockers: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, lacosamide
  • These block Nav1.1 further, exacerbating the underlying channelopathy

Current treatment algorithm:

Diagnosis: Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation + clinical presentation)
  |
  v
1L: Valproate (VPA) ± clobazam — standard backbone therapy
  |
  └── Inadequate? → Add-on therapy:
        ├── EPIDIOLEX (cannabidiol) — FDA approved 2018
        ├── FINTEPLA (fenfluramine) — FDA approved 2020
        ├── Stiripentol (DIACOMIT) — older option, limited evidence
        └── Topiramate, levetiracetam — sometimes used

Key nuance: Fintepla and Epidiolex have complementary but distinct profiles. There is no head-to-head trial. Real-world treatment decisions depend heavily on physician preference, monitoring burden tolerance, and prior therapy failure.

1.3 Fintepla in Dravet Syndrome — Clinical Evidence

The fenfluramine story: Fenfluramine was originally an appetite suppressant (Pondimin / Redux), withdrawn in 1997 due to cardiac valvulopathy. Its re-emergence as an ASM for Dravet syndrome is based on a different mechanism — serotonergic modulation (5-HT2C agonism + sigma-1 receptor activity) plus a unique MOA that is distinct from all other ASMs. The cardiac risk is managed through REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) with echocardiographic monitoring.

Pivotal trial results (Study 1 — DS):

  • Profound reduction (>75%) in convulsive seizures in a substantial proportion of patients
  • 21 or more consecutive seizure-free days — a meaningful patient outcome for a disease where daily seizures are common
  • Placebo-adjusted median percent reduction in convulsive seizure frequency: significant across dose groups
  • Drug response rate was 4x compared to placebo (Fintepla), versus 3x for Epidiolex

Long-term data (Open-Label Extension, up to 3 years):

  • Sustained seizure reduction over 3 years
  • Long-term safety and efficacy maintained
  • Over 10,000 patients treated worldwide (as of 2025-2026 messaging)

Key Fintepla DS messages (from ZoomRx SFE, ranked by effectiveness):

  1. FD11 — Unique MoA: "An MoA different from other ASMs" — consistently the most effective Fintepla DS message across both waves (ME 64% in Q1 2026)
  2. FD1 — Seizure reduction: "Profound reduction (>75%) in convulsive seizures" — most recalled, high effectiveness but declining from Wave 1
  3. FD10 — Cardiac monitoring: "Cardiac monitoring via echocardiogram can detect problems before a patient becomes symptomatic" — recall higher for DS (58%) than LGS (40%)
  4. FD2 — Seizure-free days: "21 or more consecutive seizure-free days" — highly motivating to Rx but lower recall (needs improved delivery)
  5. FD7 — 3-year sustained effect: Long-term safety and efficacy data

2. Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome (LGS) — Disease Deep Dive

2.1 Epidemiology and Disease Burden

LGS is one of the most severe and treatment-resistant childhood epilepsy syndromes:

  • Prevalence: ~1-2% of all childhood epilepsies; estimated 20,000-40,000 patients in the US
  • Onset: Typically between ages 3-5 years (later than Dravet)
  • Defining features: Multiple seizure types (tonic, atonic/"drop attacks", absence), abnormal EEG (slow spike-wave <2.5 Hz), intellectual disability
  • SUDEP risk: Mortality is higher than the general epilepsy population — a message UCB uses to underscore urgency
  • Drop seizures (tonic/atonic): The signature seizure type of LGS — patients suddenly lose muscle tone and fall, causing injury. This is a key clinical endpoint
  • Drug-resistant by definition: Most LGS patients have tried and failed multiple ASMs by the time they are candidates for Fintepla

2.2 Treatment Landscape for LGS

LGS treatment pathway:
  |
  v
1L: Valproate ± clobazam/lamotrigine — standard backbone
  |
  └── Inadequate? →
        ├── EPIDIOLEX (cannabidiol) — FDA approved 2018
        ├── FINTEPLA (fenfluramine) — FDA approved March 2022
        ├── Rufinamide (BANZEL)
        ├── Felbamate (FELBATOL)
        ├── Topiramate
        ├── Ketogenic diet
        └── VNS (vagus nerve stimulation) / surgery

2.3 Fintepla in LGS — Clinical Evidence

Pivotal trial results (Study 1503 — LGS):

  • Near 50% reduction in generalized tonic-clonic (GTC) seizures
  • Broad-spectrum efficacy that significantly reduces seizures, including life-threatening seizures
  • Response rate 3x compared to placebo
  • Does not worsen behavior, emotion, and cognition vs placebo

Key Fintepla LGS messages (from ZoomRx SFE, ranked by effectiveness in Q1 2026):

  1. FL11 — Unique MoA: "An MOA different from other ASMs" (ME 66%, highest effectiveness)
  2. FL12 — SUDEP risk: "The risk of death in patients with LGS is higher than the general epilepsy population"
  3. FL1 — Broad spectrum efficacy: "Significantly reduces seizures, including life-threatening seizures" (ME 48%, declining)
  4. FL2 — GTC reduction: "Near 50% reduction in GTCs" (ME 47%, declining)
  5. FL10 — Cardiac monitoring: "Can detect problems before a patient becomes symptomatic"

Critical trend (Q3 2025 → Q1 2026): Message effectiveness of several Fintepla LGS messages declined due to drops in motivation and believability. Specifically, FL2 (seizure reduction), FL1 (broad spectrum efficacy), and FL3 (side effects) had the biggest drops in ME. The MoA message (FL11) was the exception — it GAINED effectiveness and remains the most impactful message.

3. Fintepla vs Epidiolex — The Head-to-Head Commercial Battle

3.1 Product Comparison

Attribute Fintepla (fenfluramine) Epidiolex (cannabidiol)
Manufacturer UCB (via Zogenix) Jazz Pharmaceuticals (via GW Pharma)
Mechanism 5-HT2C agonist + sigma-1 CBD (multi-target)
FDA approvals DS (2020), LGS (2022) DS + LGS (2018), TSC (2020)
Age range >=2 years >=1 year
REMS requirement Yes (cardiac monitoring) No
Monitoring Echocardiogram (baseline + q6mo) LFTs (hepatotoxicity risk)
Cardiac risk Valvulopathy (managed via REMS) None
Hepatic risk Minimal Significant (elevated ALT/AST)
Drug interactions Minimal impact on ASM PKs Multiple (esp. with clobazam, valproate)
2025 revenue ~EUR427M ~$650-700M (estimated)
Market position Challenger (2nd mover) Market leader

3.2 ZoomRx SFE Findings — Fintepla vs Epidiolex (Q3 2025 and Q1 2026)

Sales Force Activity (Q1 2026):

  • Fintepla and Epidiolex have comparable share of voice in the combined DS+LGS space (~50/50)
  • Sales rep reach and visit frequency are similar between brands
  • During multi-indication details: Fintepla KAMs spend 61% on LGS / 39% on DS; Epidiolex reps spend 58% on LGS / 30% on DS / 12% on TSC

Overall Sales Call Quality:

  • Two-thirds of Fintepla details rated highly on overall quality, comparable to Epidiolex (LGS and DS)
  • Fintepla reps improved significantly on identifying eligible patients from Wave 1 to Wave 2
  • Fintepla's likelihood to increase prescribing improved and is now higher than Epidiolex for DS, comparable for LGS

The Comfort Gap — Fintepla's Biggest Challenge:

  • Only ~40% of HCPs are comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla (both LGS and DS)
  • Epidiolex comfort is significantly higher
  • This gap is driven by REMS/cardiac monitoring complexity, not efficacy doubts
  • Mitigation: At-Home ECHO program (sends cardiac sonographers to patients' homes) — awareness growing (~48% aware in Q1 2026, up from ~40% in Q3 2025)
  • Account Experience Specialist (AES) awareness also improving (~33% in Q1 2026, up from ~20% in Q3 2025)

Likelihood to Recommend to a Colleague:

  • LGS: 80% would recommend Fintepla (improved from prior wave)
  • DS: Comparable to Epidiolex
  • This is a leading indicator — recommendation intent is growing even while prescribing comfort lags

3.3 Message Effectiveness Trends (Q3 2025 → Q1 2026)

Fintepla's winning message: The unique MOA message (FL11/FD11 — "An MOA different from other ASMs") is consistently the most effective message for both LGS and DS. It achieves high recall AND high effectiveness, placing it in the "most impactful" quadrant. This is Fintepla's clearest differentiator from Epidiolex.

Concerning trend: Multiple Fintepla efficacy messages are declining in motivation and believability from Wave 1 to Wave 2:

  • LGS: Seizure reduction (FL2), broad spectrum efficacy (FL1), side effects (FL3) all dropped
  • DS: Profound reduction (FD1) declined in recall and effectiveness; seizure-free days (FD2) campaign dropped
  • Epidiolex efficacy messages also declined — suggesting market-wide messaging fatigue, not Fintepla-specific

Product perception improvement (Q1 2026): HCPs report improved perceptions of Fintepla across MoA, safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetic profile following sales details. Access perceptions also improved for LGS. Efficacy perceptions remain stable — Wave 3 will be the checkpoint to see if growing efficacy recall translates to perception shifts.

3.4 High Impact Details — Best Practices

From the Q1 2026 (Wave 2) analysis, successful Fintepla details share these characteristics:

  • LGS: Strong recall of the GTC seizure reduction message correlates with positive likelihood to prescribe
  • DS: Recall of the "results within weeks of reaching maximum dose" message (FD6) correlates with positive Rx intent
  • Best practices across both: Identifying patients during the detail, using visual aids, addressing questions, providing a compelling reason to prescribe, and explicitly asking for the prescription

4. CDKL5 Deficiency Disorder — The Third Indication

4.1 Disease Context

CDKL5 deficiency disorder (CDD) is an ultra-rare genetic condition:

  • Caused by mutations in the CDKL5 gene (cyclin-dependent kinase-like 5)
  • Affects primarily females (X-linked)
  • Characterized by early-onset seizures (typically within first weeks/months of life), severe developmental delay, limited motor function
  • Estimated fewer than 1,000 diagnosed patients in the US
  • No FDA-approved treatments specifically for CDD

4.2 GEMZ Trial Results (December 2025)

The Phase 3 GEMZ trial demonstrated compelling efficacy:

  • Primary endpoint met: 47.6% reduction in countable motor seizure frequency (CMSF) with Fintepla vs 2.8% reduction with placebo
  • Seizure-free days: Patients gaining a median of 6.4 additional seizure-free days per month
  • Population: 87 patients aged 1 to 35 years
  • This makes CDKL5 the third DEE where Fintepla has shown positive Phase 3 results (after DS and LGS)

4.3 Regulatory and Commercial Implications

  • UCB plans to file for regulatory approval "as soon as possible"
  • If approved, CDKL5 would be the first FDA-approved treatment for the condition
  • Small patient population but high-value (orphan drug, premium pricing)
  • Strengthens UCB's narrative as the rare epilepsy company
  • Same REMS program, same At-Home ECHO infrastructure — marginal cost to add the indication

5. Unaided Recall Patterns — What HCPs Remember

Unaided recall (what HCPs spontaneously remember after a detail, without message prompts) provides the most authentic view of what's landing:

Fintepla LGS — Most recalled topics (unaided):

  1. Efficacy and seizure control (highest for both brands)
  2. Monitoring requirements (higher for Fintepla vs Epidiolex, but declining in Q1 2026)
  3. Safety, side effects, and tolerability
  4. Dosing and administration

Fintepla DS — Most recalled topics (unaided):

  1. Efficacy and seizure control (Fintepla details stronger than Epidiolex in Q1 2026)
  2. Monitoring requirements (declining — a positive signal that focus is shifting to efficacy)
  3. Safety / side effects

Key insight: The shift in unaided recall from monitoring requirements toward efficacy/seizure control is a positive trend for Fintepla. It suggests KAMs are successfully reframing the conversation from "cardiac monitoring burden" to "clinical benefit." This shift was statistically significant from Wave 1 to Wave 2.

HCP verbatim (from details):

  • "Excellent efficacy especially for DS with many becoming seizure-free. Needs cardiac monitoring but can be ambulatory at home"
  • "Effective in controlling drop seizures, cardiac monitoring can be done at home"
  • "Company support in getting echocardiography... convenient, will prescribe more"
  • "Patient did not need to make an appointment with a cardiologist to continue with their monitoring"

These verbatims confirm: when the At-Home ECHO program is communicated effectively, the cardiac monitoring barrier drops from "deal-breaker" to "manageable inconvenience."

6. Key Numbers to Know — Fintepla / Rare Epilepsy

Metric Value Source
Fintepla 2025 global revenue EUR427M (~$501M) UCB Annual Report
Fintepla YoY growth (2025) +30% UCB Annual Report
DS drug response rate vs placebo 4x (Fintepla) vs 3x (Epidiolex) Clinical trials
LGS drug response rate vs placebo 3x (Fintepla) vs 2x (Epidiolex) Clinical trials
CDKL5 seizure reduction (GEMZ) 47.6% vs 2.8% placebo Phase 3
HCP comfort starting Fintepla ~40% ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
HCP comfort starting Epidiolex Significantly higher ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
At-Home ECHO awareness ~48% ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
AES awareness ~33% ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
Fintepla MoA message ME (LGS) 66% ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
Fintepla MoA message ME (DS) 64% ZoomRx SFE Q1 2026
Rare disease IA ME 62% ZoomRx benchmark
Patients treated worldwide >10,000 UCB messaging
